iran_crude: Iran maintains ~2.0 mb/d crude exports despite being the belligerent — Kharg Island loading continues under IRGC military protection, with volumes dipping to 1.6 mb/d (16–22 Mar) before recovering to 2.0 mb/d (23–29 Mar) as convoy escort operations stabilized.

China emerged as the sole identified buyer at 148 kbd/d in 23–29 Mar, consistent with bilateral yuan payment arrangements and IRGC-tolled Hormuz passage ($2M/vessel per NBC News). The vast majority of Iran's crude is moving to undisclosed destinations — likely dark-fleet transfers to Chinese teapot refineries.

With CENTCOM reporting 2,000+ targets destroyed and Hengam Island (inside the Strait) struck Apr 1, Iran's ability to sustain Kharg loadings depends entirely on whether the Apr 6 deadline triggers further escalation.
iran_naphtha: Iran's naphtha exports collapsed to zero after 9–15 Mar (46 kbd/d, all to UAE), as the Hormuz closure severed short-haul Gulf product trade routes. The UAE was Iran's sole naphtha buyer — highlighting how intra-Gulf petrochemical feedstock flows have been entirely eliminated by the conflict.
iraq_crude: Iraq crude exports revived to 250 kbd/d (23–29 Mar) — entirely via the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey, restarted Mar 17 after being offline since 2023. This represents a 93% collapse from Iraq's pre-war ~3.5 mb/d export capacity, with the Oil Ministry's FM on all foreign-operated fields leaving Basra output at just 800 kbd/d as storage fills.

The Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline is now Iraq's sole lifeline to global markets at 250 kbd/d maximum throughput. The BP/Castrol warehouse drone attack in Irbil (Apr 1) signals that even this route faces IRGC targeting risk — any disruption would reduce Iraq's exports to near zero.
Turkey is the exclusive customer, receiving every barrel via pipeline — a total dependency that gives Ankara significant leverage in post-conflict negotiations.
iraq_naphtha: Effectively zero — one tiny 27 kbd/d cargo to UAE appeared 30 Mar–1 Apr, likely residual stored volumes. Basra refining is at minimal throughput under FM conditions and the Kurdistan Khor Mor gas processing plant has halted, eliminating NGL-derived naphtha output.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait's crude exports have been functionally eliminated: zero in three of four weeks, with a single 78 kbd/d blip in 16–22 Mar (likely a tanker that loaded before the full blockade). KPC's FM on 1.2 mb/d of crude exports, the Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes (Mar 19–20), and the IRGC airport fuel tank attack (Apr 1, 58-hour fire) have shut the country's entire export apparatus.
With no pipeline bypass and every export terminal inside the Gulf, Kuwait is the most completely trade-isolated Gulf producer. KPC estimates 3–4 months to restore full capacity even post-war.
kuwait_gasoil_diesel: Collapsed from 102 kbd/d (9–15 Mar, half going to Qatar for domestic needs) to zero in all subsequent weeks. Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction has eliminated Kuwait's refined product export capacity — this 615 kbd refinery complex was Kuwait's sole major export refinery.
The initial Qatar-bound diesel flow (48 kbd/d) was itself a crisis indicator: Qatar receiving Kuwaiti diesel reflects the total shutdown of Qatar's own Laffan Refineries.
kuwait_gasoline: A single week of 64 kbd/d to Saudi Arabia (9–15 Mar), then zero. Same driver as diesel — Al-Ahmadi offline means no feedstock to produce exportable gasoline. Saudi Arabia briefly buying Kuwaiti gasoline (before the strikes) underscores the unusual intra-Gulf product reshuffling in the crisis's first week.
oman_crude: Oman is the crisis's biggest beneficiary: crude exports surged 144% from 550 kbd/d (9–15 Mar) to 1,340 kbd/d (30 Mar–1 Apr), driven entirely by its non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal on the Gulf of Oman. Inland production is intact at ~950 kbd/d.

China is the dominant buyer (>80% share across all weeks), with Taiwan appearing for 269 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar — likely Formosa Petrochemical seeking non-Hormuz crude to partially restart its naphtha crackers running at 50% capacity.

Oman crude is commanding a significant premium over Brent (per Kpler), and the Salalah port resumption (Mar 31, after the Mar 29 drone strike destroyed a crane) shows that even Oman is not fully insulated from IRGC targeting.
oman_lng: Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility is exporting 23–43 ktons/day, rotating between India, Thailand, Japan, Taiwan, and China. India has become the anchor buyer at 21–23 ktons/d in recent weeks — likely backfilling QatarEnergy's contracted 7.5 Mtpa to Petronet LNG (now under FM).

With Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline and Asian spot LNG prices surging ~39% (per ICIS), Oman LNG is commanding steep premiums. The facility's outside-Hormuz location makes it one of only three functioning LNG export points in the wider Gulf region (alongside US Gulf Coast and Australian NWS).
oman_kero_jet: Intermittent jet fuel exports of 25–46 kbd/d in 16–29 Mar, exclusively to Djibouti — almost certainly military/coalition logistics supply for operations in the Horn of Africa and Red Sea corridor. Zero volumes in the most recent period suggest loading schedule variability rather than a structural change.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Diesel exports nearly quadrupled from 50 kbd/d (9–15 Mar, Pakistan only) to 184 kbd/d (23–29 Mar), with the customer base broadening to include South Africa, Singapore, and Mauritius. Oman's Duqm refinery (230 kbd capacity) is filling the vacuum left by Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi and Bahrain's Sitra (405 kbd combined) going offline.

The geographic reach of Oman's diesel — Pakistan, South Africa, Mauritius, Singapore — reveals just how far the product shortage has radiated from the Gulf. These are markets that would normally source from Gulf refiners closer to the Hormuz chokepoint.
oman_gasoline: Gasoline exports tripled from 43 kbd/d (9–15 Mar) to 120 kbd/d (23–29 Mar), with Pakistan (73 kbd/d) and Saudi Arabia (47 kbd/d) emerging as buyers. Saudi Arabia purchasing gasoline from Oman is a striking crisis indicator — the kingdom is redirecting its own refinery output toward maximizing crude pipeline throughput on the constrained Yanbu system.

African markets (Tanzania, Sudan, South Africa) appeared in earlier weeks, demonstrating Oman's emergence as an alternative refined product hub for Indian Ocean markets cut off from traditional Gulf suppliers.
oman_naphtha: Declined from 72 kbd/d (9–15 Mar, South Korea) to zero by 23–29 Mar. With global naphtha surging past $1,000/mt (per ICIS) and South Korea's YNCC declaring FM on ethylene production, Oman is likely retaining feedstock for its own Sohar petrochemical complex rather than exporting at even elevated prices.
qatar_crude: Zero exports in three of four weeks; the sole blip (117 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar, half to UAE) was likely stored crude moving from floating storage to UAE's ADCOP pipeline system for re-export. All Qatar offshore oil fields (~440 kbd) have been shut since early March under QatarEnergy's blanket FM covering all hydrocarbon exports.
With Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 offline and no pipeline bypass, Qatar has zero crude export optionality until the Strait reopens.
qatar_lng: Trace volumes only — 6.5–18.4 ktons/day in 16–29 Mar to Kuwait and Pakistan, likely residual stored LNG moving via short-haul Gulf routes. Qatar's main production (77 Mtpa, ~20% of global LNG supply) has been halted since Mar 2.

The AQUA 1 tanker missile strike (Apr 1) and QatarEnergy's admission that 12.8 Mtpa of liquefaction capacity suffered physical damage mean full restart is 3–5 years away even post-conflict. FM has been extended through mid-June with Edison notified of 10 lost cargoes — this is not a pause, it is a structural removal of global LNG supply.
saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports climbed from ~4.0 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~6.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar) as the East-West Pipeline to Yanbu reached maximum capacity (~4.5 mb/d port throughput, per Argus), with 37–40 VLCCs loading at Yanbu this month alone.

Egypt dominates at 1.5 mb/d (~25% of total) — these are SUMED pipeline transshipments to Mediterranean buyers, not Egyptian consumption. China (1.1 mb/d) and India (1.0 mb/d) are the true end-customers via Red Sea routing. Myanmar appeared as a new buyer at 572 kbd/d — an unusual strategic fuel purchase.

The pipeline bypass is working but capped: even at max throughput, Yanbu recovers only ~65% of pre-crisis Ras Tanura volumes (~6.4 mb/d). South Korea and Japan are intermittent buyers — their absence in W13 suggests SPR drawdowns are replacing Saudi spot purchases. The Apr 5 OPEC+ meeting will be critical for signaling post-crisis production plans.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Volatile jet fuel exports (0–115 kbd/d) reflecting lumpy tanker loading schedules at Yanbu. UK received the largest single cargo at 95 kbd/d (16–22 Mar). Jet fuel is a lower priority than crude and diesel for constrained Red Sea pipeline capacity, keeping flows intermittent.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel exports held steady at 380–780 kbd/d with a remarkably diversified customer base: Egypt (anchor at 140–285 kbd/d), plus Kenya, Italy, Netherlands, Belgium, Turkey, Slovenia, Spain, Malaysia, Pakistan, Djibouti, and Sudan. The Red Sea route is functioning as a robust alternative to Hormuz-dependent flows.

This spread across Europe, Africa, and Asia partially fills the 1+ mb/d regional refining gap left by Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi (615 kbd) and Bahrain's Sitra (405 kbd) going offline. Saudi Yanbu refineries are the last major functioning export refineries in the Gulf region.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Gasoline exports peaked at 365 kbd/d (16–22 Mar, dominated by Singapore at 138 kbd/d) then faded to zero by 30 Mar–1 Apr, as Saudi prioritized crude and diesel volumes on constrained Yanbu pipeline capacity. The product hierarchy is clear: crude first, then diesel, with gasoline and naphtha getting residual allocation.

Customer mix shifted entirely to non-traditional markets — Singapore, Malaysia, Kenya, Sudan, South Africa — as Japan and South Korea (normally major Saudi product buyers) draw on SPR reserves instead.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Moderate naphtha exports of 73–132 kbd/d, primarily to Indonesia and Japan. With global naphtha past $1,000/mt and multiple Asian crackers declaring FM (Formosa, YNCC, Chandra Asri), Saudi is allocating NGL-derived naphtha to its own SATORP and Petro Rabigh complexes rather than exporting — a rational choice given the kingdom's domestic petrochemical revenue targets.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports swung from 2.1 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to 1.1 mb/d (16–22 Mar) back to 3.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar), reflecting ADCOP pipeline flow variability and Fujairah tanker loading schedules. ADCOP is at a record 1.6 mb/d (per Kpler), making the Habshan-Fujairah corridor the UAE's only functioning export route.

Customer base is 100% Asian: India (up to 700 kbd/d), South Korea (514 kbd/d), Japan (486 kbd/d), Thailand, Vietnam, Philippines, and Pakistan. The complete absence of European buyers reflects the Red Sea routing disadvantage versus Saudi Yanbu.

Fujairah itself is now under direct attack — UAE intercepted 5 ballistic missiles + 35 drones on Apr 1 (per Washington Post), with a Bangladeshi worker killed. Any sustained damage to Fujairah port would cut the UAE's last crude export artery, potentially removing another 1.6 mb/d from global supply.
uae_gasoil_diesel: Collapsed from 73 kbd/d (9–15 Mar, Pakistan) to zero in all subsequent weeks. Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and Shah Gas Field shut (~1 Bcf/d) have eliminated UAE's product export capacity. With neighboring Bahrain's Sitra (405 kbd) also destroyed, the lower Gulf has zero functioning export refineries.
uae_naphtha: Near-zero with a single 46 kbd/d cargo to Taiwan (23–29 Mar) — likely Formosa Petrochemical securing any available non-Hormuz naphtha as its crackers run at ~50% capacity. UAE naphtha production is severely curtailed by the Ruwais shutdown; Fujairah bunkering hub FM has further constrained downstream output.
russia_crude: Russia's crude exports declined 19% from ~6.7 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~5.5 mb/d (23-29 Mar) as Asian buyers reached absorption limits, though volumes remain the highest of any non-Gulf exporter and well above pre-crisis norms as Moscow fills the Hormuz supply vacuum.

China remains the anchor buyer at ~1.3 mb/d (23-29 Mar), down from 2.3 mb/d the prior week, while India slipped to 0.8 mb/d from 1.5 mb/d -- both are saturating storage and refining capacity after a month of surge buying. Philippines took its first ESPO cargo in six years (212 kbd, 16-22 Mar), signaling Southeast Asian diversification that per Rigzone reflects US sanctions waivers enabling energy-starved nations to tap Russian barrels.

European buyers persist despite the sanctions framework: Italy (~660 kbd), France (~307 kbd), Netherlands (~252 kbd), and Turkey (~666 kbd) all took significant volumes in 23-29 Mar, collectively absorbing ~1.9 mb/d -- more than India. Turkey in particular surged 3.5x from the prior week, likely acting as a blending/re-export hub.

Risk ahead: Russia's gasoline export ban (effective 1 Apr) and declining diesel/naphtha exports suggest Moscow is prioritizing domestic supply, which could cap crude export growth even as global demand intensifies ahead of the critical OPEC+ Apr 5 meeting.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports held at ~80-110 ktons/d across March, with Europe still absorbing the majority -- France (21-31 ktons/d), Belgium, Spain, and Netherlands are the top buyers -- racing to stock up before the EU's ban on Russian LNG purchases takes effect April 25.

China's share is volatile (9-28 ktons/d week to week) while Japan (18-21 ktons/d) and South Korea (~9 ktons/d) provide steady Asian demand, filling the gap left by Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline. Per ICIS LNG Edge, Asian spot prices have surged ~39%, making Russian cargoes competitive even at a discount to spot.

With Arctic LNG-2 still below nameplate capacity and Yamal as the primary export facility, Russia cannot materially scale LNG output to fill the ~20% global supply gap from Qatar -- the benefit is margin expansion on existing volumes, not volume growth.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports collapsed 64% from ~1,005 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~360 kbd (23-29 Mar) before rebounding to ~785 kbd in 30 Mar-1 Apr, reflecting both tightening domestic supply priorities and lumpy cargo scheduling. Turkey remains the dominant buyer at 211-362 kbd, functioning as a key transit and blending hub for Russian barrels.

Brazil emerged as a significant buyer (107-243 kbd when present) but dropped out entirely in 23-29 Mar, while new African markets (Togo, Ghana, Morocco, Benin) and conflict-affected Syria are absorbing smaller volumes. The customer mix increasingly skews toward countries with weak sanctions enforcement.

Russia's gasoline export ban from April 1 -- confirmed by zero gasoline shipments since mid-March -- signals Moscow is hoarding refined products for domestic needs as global fuel prices surge past $4/gallon in the US.
russia_gasoline: Russian gasoline exports effectively ceased after 9-15 Mar (a single 37.5 kbd cargo to Libya), with zero shipments in every subsequent week. This aligns with Moscow's announced gasoline export ban effective April 1, implemented as domestic fuel prices spiked amid the global crisis.
The ban removes ~100-150 kbd of pre-crisis Russian gasoline from global markets, compounding the supply squeeze for African and Middle Eastern buyers who had begun sourcing Russian product.
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports declined steadily from ~677 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~358 kbd (23-29 Mar) before dropping to zero in the latest partial week, as Asian petrochemical crackers that depend on Gulf-sourced naphtha scramble for alternatives. Indonesia appeared as a new buyer (115 kbd, 16-22 Mar) -- consistent with Chandra Asri's FM declaration citing Middle East feedstock loss.

China and India remain the core buyers but at shrinking volumes (China from 231 to 81 kbd, India from 155 to 51 kbd), suggesting either Russian supply constraints or competition from US naphtha cargoes now reaching Asia at steep premiums.
us_crude: US crude exports surged 14% to ~4.4 mb/d (23-29 Mar), the highest weekly rate of the crisis, as Gulf Coast loadings ramped to fill the Hormuz void -- per Bloomberg, Asia-bound April loadings reached ~60 million barrels, the most since April 2023.

Customer mix diversified dramatically: 15 countries took US crude in 23-29 Mar versus 12 two weeks earlier. New crisis-driven buyers include China (224 kbd), Australia (86 kbd), Peru (71 kbd), and Denmark (205 kbd). Per Nation Thailand, cargoes to Taiwan priced at $12-13/bbl over Brent -- unheard-of premiums reflecting desperation for non-Gulf supply.

South Korea (~306 kbd), Japan (~480 kbd), and Taiwan (~399 kbd) are the anchor Asian buyers, collectively taking ~1.2 mb/d or 27% of total US crude exports -- nearly double the pre-crisis Asian share of ~35 million barrels/month.

Europe remains critical: Netherlands surged to 904 kbd (23-29 Mar), France, UK, and Spain each taking 200-310 kbd. The transatlantic crude trade is now structurally larger than at any point since the shale export ban was lifted.
us_lng: US LNG exports held remarkably steady at ~370-400 ktons/d across all four weeks, confirming that all 8 US terminals including Sabine Pass and Corpus Christi are running at or above nameplate capacity (~110% utilization per Fortune). With Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline, the US is now the world's largest LNG exporter.

Customer distribution is broadening: 20 countries received US LNG in 9-15 Mar, including new crisis-driven buyers like Bangladesh (9-10 ktons/d), Thailand (11-34 ktons/d), and India (20-24 ktons/d). Europe still absorbs the plurality -- Netherlands, France, Germany, Spain, and Italy collectively take ~40-50% of cargoes.

The ~375 ktons/d US export rate (~2.6 Mtpa annualized per week) fills roughly a third of Qatar's lost 77 Mtpa, underscoring why Asian spot LNG prices have surged ~39%. Golden Pass LNG's first train (6 Mtpa) and Corpus Christi Train 5 (1.5 Mtpa) coming online provide incremental relief but cannot close the gap.
us_lpg: US LPG exports rose 14% to ~168 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) as Gulf Coast terminals at Enterprise and Targa fill the void left by Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL shutdowns -- the world's three largest LPG exporters are all offline or severely curtailed.

Japan remains the anchor buyer (~29-33 ktons/d every week), but the most notable shift is surging Southeast Asian demand: Indonesia doubled to 13.4 ktons/d, China appeared at 13.2 ktons/d, and Thailand/Vietnam are taking regular cargoes. Per ICIS, China's PDH plant operators are scrambling for US cargoes at sharply higher prices.
The LPG-naphtha spread now structurally favors increased US exports as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked supply constraints, supporting further volume growth into Q2.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports surged 52% from 214 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 326 kbd (16-22 Mar), with Europe emerging as the primary demand driver -- UK took 87 kbd and Netherlands 43 kbd in 23-29 Mar. This reflects the acute European jet fuel shortage after ~30% of Europe's imports were cut off by Hormuz, per Argus Media.

The customer base shifted dramatically from Latin America-dominated (Brazil, Mexico, Chile in W11) to Europe-dominated (UK, Netherlands absorbing 60%+ by 30 Mar-1 Apr). Jet crack spreads in Europe remain at $65-68/bbl, well above the Singapore peak of $40/bbl, pulling US cargoes east across the Atlantic.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports surged 56% to ~1,871 kbd (23-29 Mar), the highest weekly rate of the crisis, driven by a massive ramp in European buying -- Netherlands alone took 354 kbd, UK 129 kbd, and new buyers Spain and Morocco appeared. This followed the EU Commission's call for member states to curb demand and postpone refinery maintenance.

Latin America and Africa remain the volume base (Peru 187 kbd, Chile 108 kbd, Australia 87 kbd, South Africa 43 kbd), but the structural shift toward European buyers signals that EU refineries cannot replace Gulf-sourced middle distillates domestically despite the Commission's efforts.
Australia appeared as a diesel buyer (43-100 kbd across all weeks) for the first time in the dataset, per Export Finance Australia's new underwriting facility for fuel imports amid the Hormuz crisis.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports remained stable at ~690-826 kbd, almost entirely directed at the Western Hemisphere -- Mexico (~148-338 kbd), Colombia, Ecuador, and Central American nations dominate. Unlike diesel and jet, gasoline has not seen a crisis-driven geographic reshuffling.

Notable exceptions: Japan appeared as a buyer (46 kbd, 23-29 Mar) and Australia took 87 kbd (16-22 Mar), both unprecedented -- reflecting that even gasoline-sufficient nations are building precautionary stockpiles amid uncertainty over when Gulf flows resume. US domestic prices at $4/gallon may limit further export growth.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports surged 81% to 759 kbd (16-22 Mar) before settling at ~546 kbd (23-29 Mar), driven by South Korea's panic buying -- S. Korea took 251 kbd in a single week as its naphtha crackers face feedstock starvation after the Gulf shutdown cut off 77% of its naphtha imports, per ICIS.

Japan (75-114 kbd) and Venezuela (~64-102 kbd) are steady buyers, while Brazil (57-87 kbd) provides Latin American demand. The crisis has turned the US from a secondary naphtha supplier to a critical lifeline for Asian petrochemical complexes, with naphtha prices past $1,000/mt globally.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude exports are minimal and structurally irrelevant (~46-198 kbd), limited to intra-European transfers (Finland, UK, Netherlands) and a single Turkey cargo. The EU is a net crude importer and these small re-export flows reflect refinery balancing, not a crisis response.
Zero Gulf exposure in any week -- as expected for an import-dependent bloc with no Middle Eastern crude re-export infrastructure.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU jet fuel exports collapsed 77% from ~146 kbd (9-15 Mar) to just ~34 kbd (23-29 Mar) as the bloc retains barrels for domestic aviation amid the most severe jet fuel shortage since Hormuz closed. Per Kpler, ~30% of Europe's jet imports normally transit the Gulf, and jet crack spreads are holding at $65-68/bbl.

The bounce to ~173 kbd (30 Mar-1 Apr) is driven by Netherlands-to-Netherlands/France/Portugal intra-EU transfers, not genuine export recovery. The European Commission's March 31 directive to postpone refinery maintenance is aimed at keeping middle distillate output maximized.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU diesel exports halved from ~1,120-1,180 kbd (9-22 Mar) to ~608 kbd (23-29 Mar), a clear signal that the bloc is retaining refined products for domestic needs. Poland, historically the top intra-EU re-exporter, fell from 245 kbd to 49 kbd as Eastern European supply tightens.

The customer base shifted from broad distribution (28 destinations in 9-15 Mar) to concentrated regional flows (20 destinations in 23-29 Mar), with Africa (Morocco, Mauritania) and Mediterranean neighbors (Croatia, Slovenia) absorbing smaller volumes. The EU is structurally transitioning from a diesel exporter to a net importer as the crisis drags on.

New buyers like Lebanon (30-56 kbd) and Egypt (13-93 kbd) reflect Middle Eastern nations that can no longer source from Gulf refineries, adding emergency demand onto an already stressed European refining system.
eu_27_gasoline: EU gasoline exports bucked the diesel trend, rising 27% to ~1,425 kbd (23-29 Mar) -- the highest weekly rate in the dataset -- as the bloc has relative surplus in gasoline versus acute shortage in middle distillates. Africa is the dominant growth market: Kenya (111 kbd), Egypt (151 kbd), Ghana (49 kbd), and South Africa (147 kbd) collectively took ~460 kbd.

Saudi Arabia appeared as a buyer in 9-15 Mar (37 kbd) -- a striking reversal that underscores how Gulf producers with shuttered refineries (Bapco Sitra offline, Aramco refinery throughput cuts) now need to import finished fuels.
The US appeared as a buyer at 79-108 kbd in multiple weeks, an unusual transatlantic gasoline flow that reflects regional dislocations in the global product market.
eu_27_naphtha: EU naphtha exports remained stable at ~355-413 kbd through 23-29 Mar but the customer base shifted dramatically toward Asia: Japan surged to 103 kbd (23-29 Mar) and China appeared at 49 kbd, reflecting the desperate search for petrochemical feedstock after Gulf naphtha supplies were severed.

South Korea (73 kbd in 9-15 Mar) was an early buyer -- consistent with YNCC's FM declaration citing total Middle East naphtha suspension. Naphtha prices above $1,000/mt are making long-haul EU-to-Asia shipments economic for the first time in years.

The 30 Mar-1 Apr period collapsed to just 93 kbd (Spain only), potentially signaling EU refineries are starting to retain naphtha for domestic petrochemical use as LyondellBasell's FM on European polyolefins compounds feedstock competition within the bloc.
australia_crude: Australia's crude exports are small (~183-280 kbd) and entirely directed at Asia-Pacific neighbors -- South Korea is the anchor buyer at 93-146 kbd, with Singapore, Thailand, Brunei, and Japan rotating as secondary buyers.

The steady volumes reflect Australia's modest ~300 kbd production base, which is outside the Gulf disruption zone and commanding premium pricing. Oman and Australia are the only significant Asia-facing crude exporters with no Hormuz exposure, giving both outsized pricing power.
australia_lng: Australia's LNG exports declined 33% from ~252 ktons/d (9-15 Mar) to ~169 ktons/d (23-29 Mar), compounding the global shortage at the worst possible time -- this drop coincides with the Santos-operated Barossa/Darwin LNG temporary shutdown reported by IBTimes. Japan remains the anchor buyer at ~71 ktons/d, unwavering.

Per the Diplomat, Australia and Singapore signed an agreement to maintain oil and gas flows, reflecting the strategic importance of Australian LNG with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline. The customer base is exclusively Asian -- Japan, China, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, and Singapore -- with no European cargoes.

At ~169-185 ktons/d (annualized ~62-68 Mtpa), Australia is running well below its ~87 Mtpa nameplate capacity. If the Barossa outage extends or other facilities face maintenance issues, the Asia-Pacific LNG supply picture deteriorates further against Daiwa Institute's warning that a 10% cut to Japan's ME LNG imports would push GDP negative.
australia_naphtha: Australia's naphtha exports are negligible (~49 kbd sporadically), with only Singapore as a buyer. Australia's limited refining infrastructure means it has minimal naphtha surplus for export -- the country is itself increasingly import-dependent for refined products amid the crisis.
china_crude: China's crude imports fell 30% from ~11.4 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~7.7 mb/d (30 Mar–1 Apr), yet Beijing appears relatively calm — per The Diplomat, China's 1.2–1.3 billion barrel strategic/commercial reserve offers ~100 days of cover, and pipeline-delivered crude (ESPO, Kazakhstan, Myanmar ~1,020 kbd) provides a Hormuz-independent floor.

Russia held steady at ~2.2 mb/d throughout the crisis while Saudi Arabia disappeared entirely by 23–29 Mar; Brazil surged to become the top supplier at 2.2 mb/d in early April, with Angola (1.4 mb/d) also filling the gap — a radical reshuffling of China's supply base in under four weeks.

Gulf share collapsed from 44% to 14%, but Iran continues flowing ~1.1 mb/d to China — Tehran reportedly allows China-bound vessels through the Hormuz blockade, giving Beijing a geopolitical hedge unavailable to Japan or South Korea.

With the OPEC+ April 5 meeting looming as the most consequential since the alliance's formation, China's ability to maintain ~7.7 mb/d through diversification and reserves buys time, but any prolongation past April would test even Beijing's stockpile depth.
china_lng: China's LNG imports collapsed 39% to 81.7 ktons/d in 23–29 Mar as QatarEnergy's FM eliminated China's largest Gulf supplier — Qatar fell from 45.2 ktons/d to zero by mid-March, consistent with the FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24.

Australia emerged as the lifeline supplier (25.6–58.1 ktons/d), while Oman's non-Hormuz terminal began delivering 25.6 ktons/d in 30 Mar–1 Apr — the first Gulf LNG to reach China in two weeks, signaling a partial recovery.

Asian LNG spot prices surged ~39% per Bloomberg Intelligence and Europe is losing cargoes as traders divert flexible supply eastward per Euronews — China's Power of Siberia pipeline gas provides partial insulation, but the loss of Qatar's 20% global share is driving industrial gas users toward costly coal-switching.
china_lpg: US Gulf Coast LPG dominates China's import mix at ~28 ktons/d average — double the next supplier — as Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL-linked LPG dried up; China, the world's largest LPG importer, faces sharply higher PDH feedstock costs per ICIS.

Iran remains a notable LPG supplier to China at 10–22 ktons/d, exploiting IRGC toll-collection transit through Hormuz (~$2M/vessel in yuan per Vortexa), while Gulf share oscillated between 9% and 49% depending on Iranian flow timing.

Total imports fell 31% from 59 to 41 ktons/d by early April; further tightening is likely as Saudi and UAE NGL production remains offline and competition for US cargoes intensifies with Japan and South Korea.
china_kero_jet: China's jet fuel/kerosene imports effectively ceased after a single 24 kbd cargo from South Korea in 9–15 Mar, reflecting China's domestic fuel export ban (since Mar 4–5) and the broader collapse in regional refined product trade — self-sufficiency in jet fuel limits direct impact, but aviation demand destruction from $105+ Brent is the bigger concern.
china_gasoil_diesel: China's gasoil/diesel imports — already minimal at ~12–15 kbd — are sourced entirely from intra-Asian trade (South Korea, Japan) with zero Gulf exposure; volumes edged to zero by 30 Mar–1 Apr as regional refinery throughput cuts constrained available exports.
china_naphtha: China's naphtha imports collapsed 71% from 409 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 119 kbd (30 Mar–1 Apr) as Gulf suppliers — Kuwait, Qatar, UAE — disappeared entirely by 23–29 Mar, with Gulf share crashing from 46% to zero.

Russia became the sole naphtha supplier by early April at 119 kbd, consistent with Shell-CNOOC's Huizhou ethylene cracker shutdown and Wanhua Chemical's FM on Middle East supplies — both reducing feedstock demand even as available supply dried up.

With naphtha past $1,000/mt and 3,200 Chinese-linked vessels trapped in the Gulf (4% of global tonnage per S&P Global), further cracker shutdowns are likely; Formosa Plastics in Taiwan already cut to ~50% capacity on the same Gulf naphtha disruption.
india_crude: India's crude imports recovered from 3.6 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to 4.2 mb/d (23–29 Mar), supported by Operation Urja Suraksha — the Indian Navy's frontline warship escort of India-bound tankers through Hormuz — which enabled Saudi (0.95 mb/d) and UAE (0.49 mb/d) deliveries to resume mid-month.

Russia is India's dominant supplier at 53% average share across March, surging to an extraordinary 3.9 mb/d (65%) in 30 Mar–1 Apr; India-Russia talks on expanded crude purchases restarted Mar 19, cementing Moscow as New Delhi's indispensable energy partner during the crisis.

Gulf share swung wildly (6%→25%→36%→0%) week by week, reflecting the convoy-dependent nature of Hormuz deliveries; Iran's 'friendly nation' safe-passage status gives India a diplomatic edge, but the trade deficit is set to widen by >$4B in March alone per Business Today.

The diversification to Brazil (0.9 mb/d), US (0.5 mb/d), and West African crudes in early April shows India building redundancy, but with MRPL cutting 500 kbd in refinery throughput, the downstream impact is already cascading through the economy.
india_lng: India's LNG imports were volatile (35→31→79→66 ktons/d) as Petronet LNG's FM on Qatar contracts removed 41% of India's pre-crisis LNG supply; Qatar has been completely absent from the supply chain since early March.

Oman's non-Hormuz Qalhat terminal provided a critical 32 ktons/d lifeline in 23–29 Mar, while Nigeria (17–20 ktons/d), Angola, and US spot cargoes rotate as replacement suppliers — but total volumes remain well below India's ~100+ ktons/d pre-crisis run rate.

Gujarat Gas imposed 50% industrial supply cuts while the Petroleum Ministry assured 'sufficient stock' on Mar 27 — India is negotiating Russian LNG purchases after the Puri-Sorokin meeting, but pipeline gas from Russia remains years away, leaving acute spot price exposure (~39% surge).
india_lpg: India's LPG imports are being sustained by a two-track model: US Gulf Coast cargoes (8–16 ktons/d baseline) plus Indian Navy-escorted Gulf deliveries — tankers Pine Gas and Jag Vasant (92,000t combined) successfully transited Hormuz under Operation Urja Suraksha, arriving at Indian ports Mar 26–28.

Gulf suppliers (Qatar, Saudi, Kuwait) continue providing 29–61% of LPG volumes week to week, enabled by the naval escort framework, and the W14 surge to 28.9 ktons/d suggests the model is scaling up — but sustained high volumes require continued naval commitment.

Cooking fuel LPG is politically critical in India; any sustained shortfall would have domestic electoral consequences, making the Navy's Hormuz escort mission as much a political imperative as a strategic one.
india_naphtha: Russia has become India's near-monopoly naphtha supplier, surging from 53 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 275 kbd (30 Mar–1 Apr) — a 5x increase — as UAE supply disappeared and Indian refiners desperately sought feedstock after MRPL's FM on gasoline exports.

Oman is the only Gulf source still delivering naphtha to India (~46–68 kbd), leveraging its non-Hormuz export position; total naphtha imports actually surged 237% from W11 to W14 as refiners compete for any available barrels.

The Russia+Oman duopoly creates dangerous concentration risk — any disruption to either source would cascade through India's petrochemical and refining sectors; diversification to Middle Eastern alternatives is impossible while Hormuz remains contested.
japan_crude: Japan remains the most Gulf-dependent major importer at 89–100% Gulf share across all four weeks — zero diversification to Russia, Brazil, or Africa — reflecting deeply entrenched long-term contracts with Saudi Arabia and UAE that leave no alternative supply infrastructure.

Total imports fell 47% from 2.0 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to 1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar), the lowest weekly level since 2016 per Vortexa, forcing Japan's largest-ever SPR release of 80 mb from 10 national stockpile sites — with METI warning additional releases may be needed past April.

Saudi and UAE are the only meaningful suppliers; Iraq, Kuwait, Oman, and Qatar provide trace volumes at best. With onshore crude inventories 9% below the seasonal average and ME dependence at 93.5%, Japan faces the most acute crude supply emergency among major Asian importers — every day of continued Hormuz disruption accelerates the SPR drawdown clock.
japan_lng: Japan's LNG imports declined 53% from 201 to 94 ktons/d across March, driven by QatarEnergy's FM and thinning spot availability as Asian JKM prices surged ~39%; the Daiwa Institute warns even a 10% ME LNG import cut would push Japan's GDP negative — current cuts far exceed that threshold.

Australia remains the critical lifeline (24–111 ktons/d) but cannot fully replace lost Gulf volumes; Canada emerged as a surprise major supplier at 48 ktons/d in 30 Mar–1 Apr, while Qatar dwindled from 10.7 ktons/d to zero.

Europe is losing LNG cargoes as traders divert flexible supply to higher-priced Asian markets per Euronews — Japan's only structural relief lies in accelerating US LNG spot purchases, which Cheniere and other US terminals are running at 110%+ nameplate capacity to accommodate.
japan_lpg: Japan has completely pivoted to US LPG, with the United States supplying virtually 100% of imports across all four weeks; volumes actually increased from 26.6 to 60.9 ktons/d, suggesting active strategic stockbuilding ahead of potential extended disruptions.

Zero Gulf LPG is reaching Japan — a complete structural break from pre-crisis patterns that favored Saudi and UAE NGL exports; the US Gulf Coast's Enterprise and Targa export terminals are the sole supply artery for Japan's residential heating and petrochemical feedstock needs.
japan_kero_jet: Japan's jet fuel/kerosene imports are sourced entirely from South Korea, rising from 31 to 115 kbd across March — South Korea's refining complex is functioning as Japan's de facto refined products backstop, with Seoul's naphtha export ban (Mar 27) not yet constraining distillate product flows.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Japan's gasoil/diesel imports are small (10–53 kbd) and sourced entirely from intra-Asian trade (South Korea and Brunei), with zero Gulf exposure; regional refining cooperation is managing this segment without material disruption.
japan_gasoline: Japan's gasoline imports surged from 27 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 85 kbd (23–29 Mar), sourced almost entirely from South Korea, before India emerged as a new supplier at 66 kbd in 30 Mar–1 Apr — a novel India→Japan gasoline trade route created by the crisis as Indian refiners (despite MRPL's FM) redirect exportable surplus to premium Asian buyers.

The tripling of gasoline imports reflects domestic refinery throughput cuts driven by crude supply shortfalls; Japan's downstream sector is increasingly dependent on regional neighbors to maintain fuel supply to consumers.
japan_naphtha: Japan's naphtha imports swung wildly (86–317 kbd) with Gulf share oscillating from 41% to 100% (a single Qatar cargo in 16–22 Mar) to 32% — Kuwait disappeared entirely while UAE maintained erratic deliveries, reflecting the convoy-dependent nature of Gulf naphtha trade.

South Korea (115 kbd) and the US (99 kbd) emerged as key non-Gulf sources in 30 Mar–1 Apr, alongside UAE's persistent but diminishing volumes — a fragile patchwork for Japan's petrochemical sector that remains fundamentally dependent on Gulf NGL-derived naphtha.
south_korea_crude: South Korea suffered the most extreme crude import contraction among major Asian importers, plunging 79% from 3.2 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to 0.66 mb/d (30 Mar–1 Apr), with the US as the sole supplier in early April — an unprecedented 100% concentration on a single source.

Gulf share collapsed from 76% to 0% in four weeks as Saudi, Iraq, Kuwait, and UAE deliveries dried up; the government launched an SPR swap on Mar 31, but with ME crude accounting for 77% of pre-crisis imports, the structural gap is enormous.

The petrochemical sector is devastated: Lotte Chemical shut Yeosu (restart not until May 29), LG Chem shut Yeosu No. 2, and YNCC declared FM on ethylene — with inventories 10% above seasonal norms from earlier stockbuilding providing the only cushion before the OPEC+ April 5 meeting outcome.
south_korea_lng: South Korea's LNG position is the most resilient in the batch — KOGAS reports inventory exceeding mandatory reserves, and Qatar's pre-crisis share was only ~14% of 2026 imports, cushioning the blow of the QatarEnergy FM compared to Japan or India.

Australia (43–67 ktons/d) and Malaysia (20–43 ktons/d) anchor the supply base; new long-term contracts (Woodside 0.5 Mtpa/10.5yr commenced 2026; TotalEnergies 1 Mtpa from end-2027) provide structural support that pre-dated the crisis.

Gulf share fell from 8% to 0% by 23–29 Mar, with Russia (21 ktons/d) appearing in early April — the broad pre-crisis diversification insulates Korea's gas supply far better than Japan, whose LNG imports fell 53% over the same period.
south_korea_lpg: South Korea receives 100% of LPG imports from North America (US ~80%, Canada ~20%) with zero Gulf exposure throughout March — a pre-crisis diversification that proved prescient as Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL supply collapsed region-wide.

Total volumes are modest (9–39 ktons/d) and adequate for remaining petrochemical operations, though the far more severe naphtha shortage is the binding constraint for Korea's cracker fleet — LPG as alternative feedstock is limited by cracker design.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: Small intra-Asian gasoil/diesel trade volumes (7–65 kbd) from Japan, China, and Russia reflect mutual refining support among Northeast Asian neighbors; the segment has zero Gulf exposure and is operating without material disruption.
south_korea_gasoline: Gasoline imports collapsed from 108 kbd (9–15 Mar, from Japan) to zero by 23–29 Mar, reflecting South Korea's sufficient domestic refining capacity and the naphtha export ban (Mar 27) redirecting feedstock to domestic fuel production over petrochemical use.
south_korea_naphtha: South Korea faces the most severe naphtha crisis in Asia: Gulf share crashed from 96% (UAE, Qatar, Bahrain supplying 469 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to 0% in four weeks, devastating the country's petrochemical sector where ME accounts for 77% of naphtha imports.

Naphtha surging past $1,000/mt triggered cascading shutdowns — Lotte Chemical (Yeosu), LG Chem (Yeosu No. 2), YNCC FM on ethylene — while the government imposed a naphtha export ban on Mar 27 and Korea signed the UK 35-nation Hormuz reopening joint statement.

Replacement sources (US 109, Algeria 100, Russia 84, Greece 69 kbd in 30 Mar–1 Apr) are smaller, fragmented, and far more expensive than pre-crisis Gulf NGL-derived naphtha; restoring Korea's $60B+ petrochemical export sector requires Hormuz reopening — alternative supply routes are structurally insufficient.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports fell 39% from ~1.25 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~0.76 mb/d (23–29 Mar) as the Hormuz closure progressively choked supply — but the composition shifted dramatically: Gulf share surged from 29% to 93% by 23–29 Mar as UAE cargoes (via ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah, outside Hormuz) became virtually the only supply available after US, Brazilian, and Libyan cargoes dried up.

Bangkok's bilateral transit deal with Tehran on Mar 28 — putting Thailand alongside India, Pakistan, and Malaysia on Iran's 'friendly nation' list — came as a Bangchak tanker anchored since Mar 11 finally cleared the Strait. Deputy PM Phiphat is also opening talks on Russian crude purchases, signaling Thailand's willingness to diversify beyond traditional Western-aligned supply chains.

Early W14 data (30 Mar–1 Apr) shows a worrying drop to ~0.41 mb/d with supply fragmenting to Indonesia and Nigeria — suggesting the Iran transit deal hasn't yet translated into sustained flow. With Thailand having limited strategic reserves and declining domestic Gulf of Thailand production, the IEA's warning of a worsening April supply crunch applies acutely here.
thailand_lng: Thailand's LNG imports have remained resilient at 25–60 ktons/d throughout March, with zero Qatar dependence — a structural advantage over neighbors like Japan and Taiwan. Australia is the anchor supplier (~20 ktons/d consistently), supplemented by a remarkably diverse mix: Canada, Mozambique, Malaysia, Oman, China, and US cargoes all appeared across the four weeks.

The first US LNG cargo arrived in the 30 Mar–1 Apr window (23.6 ktons/d), alongside Chinese re-exports (23.4 ktons/d) — reflecting Cheniere's status as the world's largest LNG exporter with Qatar offline and the scramble to place spot cargoes in premium Asian markets where prices have surged ~39%.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Thailand's diesel imports are small (~8–46 kbd) and entirely sourced from regional Asian refineries (Malaysia, India, China, South Korea) with zero Gulf exposure. The spike to 45.5 kbd in 16–22 Mar from India likely reflects opportunistic purchasing as Indian refiners — despite MRPL's FM on gasoline — redirected diesel volumes to capture regional premiums.
thailand_gasoline: Gasoline imports tripled from 5.4 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 15.7 kbd (23–29 Mar) as domestic refinery throughput declined in step with crude supply cuts, forcing Thailand to lean on Singapore and Malaysia's refining hubs. The early W14 surge to 49.4 kbd signals accelerating dependence on product imports — a costly shift with crack spreads elevated across Asia.

South Korea appeared as a new gasoline supplier in 30 Mar–1 Apr (6.7 kbd), suggesting Thailand is casting a wider net as Singapore's export capacity tightens amid region-wide demand for refined products.
thailand_naphtha: Naphtha imports collapsed from 36.7 kbd (9–15 Mar, 100% from Bahrain's Sitra refinery) to effectively zero — a direct casualty of Bapco's FM after the refinery was struck and its 405 kb/d capacity taken offline. Only a single small Singapore cargo (3.2 kbd) arrived in 23–29 Mar.

This is an existential threat to Thailand's petrochemical sector: with naphtha past $1,000/mt globally and Gulf supply eliminated, Thai crackers face the same feedstock crisis that has already forced FPCC (Taiwan) and YNCC (South Korea) into force majeure.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam's crude imports collapsed from ~286 kbd (9–15 Mar, 100% Kuwait) to zero by 23–29 Mar — the most complete supply wipeout of any importer in this crisis. Kuwait's KPC FM and the Hormuz closure severed ~80% of Vietnam's crude lifeline in a single stroke; a lone Nigerian cargo (88 kbd, 16–22 Mar) was the only non-Gulf barrel to arrive.

Nghi Son Refinery, which supplies ~40% of Vietnam's petroleum products and was built specifically for Kuwaiti crude, reports sufficient stock through end-May after the government mobilized 4 million emergency barrels. PM Chinh's emergency Decree 72/2026 slashing MFN oil tariffs to 0% and direct calls to Kuwait's PM signal the severity — Vietnam has also asked Japan and South Korea for help accessing alternative crude sources.

The zero-import weeks are unsustainable. Vietnam is negotiating with Russia and has expressed interest in US and West African grades, but these are months-long shipping routes. Without Hormuz reopening, Vietnam faces a product-import dependency spiral as refinery runs drop.
vietnam_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports held steady at 17–27 kbd, sourced entirely from China and Singapore with zero Gulf exposure. Singapore entered as a second supplier in 23–29 Mar (13.3 kbd alongside China's 13.7 kbd), providing geographic diversification. These volumes are modest and manageable — Vietnam's aviation sector is small relative to its growing refined-product deficit.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports nearly tripled from 77 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 218 kbd (23–29 Mar) as crude-starved refineries cut runs — a textbook downstream cascade where lost crude supply converts into surging product import demand. South Korea dominates at 158 kbd/d in the latest full week, effectively becoming Vietnam's diesel lifeline.

New suppliers are appearing: Taiwan shipped 48 kbd in 23–29 Mar and India emerged at 93 kbd in early W14 — reflecting the regional product reshuffle as Middle East refined exports vanish and every Asian refiner with spare capacity finds premium outlets. Vietnam's emergency tariff cuts to 0% are directly enabling these flows.
vietnam_gasoline: Gasoline imports declined from 43 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 23 kbd (23–29 Mar), sourced exclusively from Singapore's refining hub with a Japanese cargo appearing only in W11. The falling volumes are counter-intuitive amid a crude supply crisis — likely reflecting both demand destruction (higher fuel prices curbing consumption) and Singapore's own tightening product availability.
Vietnam's fuel contingency plan, activated in mid-March, includes strategic reserve drawdowns and rationing measures that may be suppressing import signals in the data.
vietnam_naphtha: After three consecutive zero-import weeks, a large Russian naphtha cargo arrived in 30 Mar–1 Apr at 113 kbd — a striking signal of Vietnam's pivot toward Russian supply enabled by the US sanctions waiver on 30 Russia-linked tankers in Asian waters (valid through Apr 11).

This is likely a one-off spot purchase rather than a sustainable supply chain, but it demonstrates how the Hormuz crisis is rewiring Asian petrochemical feedstock trade. With the sanctions waiver expiry approaching Apr 11, the window for follow-on Russian cargoes is narrow unless extended.
united_states_crude: US crude imports declined 23% from ~3.1 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~2.4 mb/d (23–29 Mar), but the supplier diversification is remarkable: 13 countries delivered crude in W13, with Venezuela (473 kbd) and Canada (173 kbd) anchoring a Western Hemisphere supply chain alongside Mexico, Guyana, Brazil, Colombia, Argentina, Ecuador, and even Ghana.

Gulf barrels (Saudi + Iraq) persisted at ~654 kbd in 23–29 Mar — Saudi crude flowing via the East-West Pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea, and Iraqi crude via the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline through Turkey. These non-Hormuz pipeline bypass routes are keeping Gulf barrels available to US Gulf Coast refineries, though Saudi volumes fell from 589 to 485 kbd over the month.

With US gasoline at $4/gal and the Brent-WTI spread collapsing to 2026 lows on de-escalation signals, the critical question is whether the OPEC+ Apr 5 meeting — the most pivotal since the group's formation — will release additional barrels or hold discipline. EIA forecasts Brent above $95/bbl through May before falling below $80 if the crisis eases.
united_states_kero_jet: South Korea is the dominant jet fuel supplier to the US, delivering 60–217 kbd across all four weeks. The volumes are volatile — dipping to 60 kbd in 16–22 Mar then surging to 217 kbd in early W14 — reflecting cargo scheduling rather than structural shifts. Canada provides a consistent 24–35 kbd baseload.

Zero Gulf exposure in US jet fuel imports provides insulation, though the global supply tightness (with Middle East refinery shutdowns from Bapco, KPC, and FPCC reducing output) puts upward pressure on Asian jet fuel cracks, which will eventually pass through to US airline costs.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: Canada dominates US diesel imports at 89–178 kbd, providing 80–100% of volumes across all four weeks via pipeline and cross-border flows. The addition of Mexico, Argentina, and South Korea as marginal suppliers reflects the global hunt for distillate barrels, but the Canada-centric supply chain is structurally insulated from Hormuz.

Zero Gulf diesel exposure, combined with the US being a major diesel exporter in normal times, means the domestic supply-side risk is minimal — the price impact is entirely a function of global crack spread contagion rather than physical shortages.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports declined 24% from 371 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 283 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Canada holding steady at ~98 kbd while other sources fluctuated. Notably, India appeared as a gasoline supplier at 44–49 kbd in weeks 12–13, likely redirected MRPL volumes seeking premium outlets despite India's own FM declarations on gasoline exports.

The Caribbean supply chain (Bahamas, USVI) remains active at 74–131 kbd, providing nearby, fast-turnaround barrels that help buffer East Coast demand. With US gas at $4/gal heading into summer driving season, these import volumes will be closely watched alongside SPR releases (45.2 mb delivered of 172 mb authorized).
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha imports shifted from European sources (Belgium, Netherlands, France at 67 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to South American and African origins (Angola 49 kbd, Brazil 36 kbd in 23–29 Mar) — likely reflecting LyondellBasell's FM on European polyolefins freeing up European naphtha for export, while South American barrels arrived as the supply chain reoriented.
The volumes are modest (46–96 kbd) and the US is largely self-sufficient in naphtha via domestic refining, so this dataset reflects marginal arbitrage flows rather than a supply security concern.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan's crude imports collapsed 66% from ~864 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just ~294 kbd (23–29 Mar) as Gulf suppliers disappeared one by one: Kuwait dropped out after W11 (KPC FM), Saudi Arabia after W12, leaving the United States as Taiwan's sole crude supplier at ~294 kbd. This is the most concentrated single-source dependency of any major importer in the crisis.

The W14 partial data shows a recovery to ~698 kbd (all US), suggesting larger US cargoes are being arranged. But Taiwan's refining capacity (~1.2 mb/d nameplate) is running far below potential, directly constraining FPCC's naphtha cracker operations already at 50% and threatening cascading shutdowns across the island's petrochemical complex.

Taiwan's extreme energy vulnerability — 97.7% import-dependent, just 11 days of gas reserves, and over a third of gas supply routed through Hormuz — has prompted President Lai to announce restarting shuttered nuclear reactors, marking a historic reversal of the DPP's nuclear-free policy.
taiwan_lng: Taiwan executed a rapid LNG diversification away from Qatar (26 ktons/d in 9–15 Mar → zero by W14), with Australia emerging as the dominant supplier at 22–51 ktons/d and accounting for over half of imports by 30 Mar–1 Apr. New sources including Equatorial Guinea, Oman (outside Hormuz), and US cargoes filled the gap left by QatarEnergy's FM.

With just 11 days of gas reserves — the lowest in East Asia — and gas-fired plants providing 53% of electricity, Taiwan's Ministry of Economic Affairs says it has secured alternative LNG through April. The rising trajectory (68→76 ktons/d across the month) suggests procurement is keeping pace, but any cargo delay or bid failure could trigger power rationing within days.

Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility, positioned outside the Hormuz chokepoint, has become a critical swing supplier commanding significant spot premiums — Taiwan drew 10 ktons/d from Oman in both W12 and W13.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports fell sharply from 97 kbd (9–15 Mar, from South Korea) to 18 kbd (23–29 Mar), reflecting both reduced domestic demand as industrial activity slows and FPCC's lower refinery runs reducing diesel output. Russia appeared as a supplier in W12 (3.8 kbd) and dominated W14 (49.7 kbd) — another signal of Asia's growing Russian product dependency.

The shift from South Korean to Russian and Chinese diesel reflects both price arbitrage (Russian barrels at discount) and South Korea's own tightening supply as Lotte Chemical halted its Yeosu plant and the naphtha export ban redirects Korean refinery output toward domestic use.
taiwan_gasoline: Gasoline imports are negligible — a single Japanese cargo of 25 kbd in 16–22 Mar was the only flow across four weeks. Taiwan is largely self-sufficient in gasoline from CPC Corporation's refineries, and lower domestic driving demand amid the energy crisis is further reducing any import need.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan's naphtha imports collapsed from 147 kbd (9–15 Mar, 100% Gulf — UAE 128 kbd + Iraq 20 kbd) to absolute zero by 23–29 Mar. This is the most consequential commodity disruption for Taiwan's economy: FPCC's Mailiao crackers are now at 50% capacity, Formosa Plastics declared FM on multiple ethylene products effective Apr 1, and Formosa Chemicals followed on Mar 25.

The wipeout is absolute — UAE and Iraq were Taiwan's only naphtha suppliers, and both require Hormuz transit. With naphtha past $1,000/mt globally and no alternative supply appearing in the data, Taiwan's petrochemical sector faces potential full shutdown. The Atlantic Council has called this a test of Taiwan's energy resilience, and the cascading PVC shortage is already disrupting global construction supply chains.
This dataset is the clearest illustration of chokepoint risk in the entire crisis: a $30+ billion petrochemical industry reduced to zero feedstock in under three weeks.
